segments.text: Here, we note that the slope of the leading front of the thickness pro /uniFB01 le in such structures is determined by the reactivity of the reactant that penetrates the structure the least deep.
Elongated openings in the membrane allow for diffusion of the ALD reactants into the lateral trench up to a depth of 5 mm, corresponding to an AR of 10 000.
In such an extremely high AR feature, the reactants typically do not reach the end of the feature during deposition, resulting in a diffusion-limited thickness pro /uniFB01 le.

The penetration depth z (m) is normalized by the total structure length L (m) such that ξ ; z = L and the dimensionless time is given by τ ; tD = L 2 .

14,36 The parameter α describes the ratio between the reaction rate and diffusion rate, while γ describes the number of reactant molecules simultaneously present in the structure per adsorption site.
For a typical feature size (e.g., a trench with a cavity height h of 500 nm) and process pressure (e.g., 100 mTorr), θ changes much slower than ~ n as γ /C28 1.

During this longer saturation time, the reactant diffuses deeper into the feature, resulting in a broader diffusion front.
For relatively high values of s 0 , e.g., 10 /C0 2 , the surface saturates quickly when the reactant diffuses inward, resulting in a sharp pro /uniFB01 le front.
For the axes in Fig. 1, a trench structure is assumed and the penetration depth is normalized by the cavity height such that ~ z ; z = h .
For example, EAR ¼ ð z = 2 h Þ ¼ ð ~ z = 2 Þ for a trench, while EAR ¼ z = r for a pore with radius r .
This pro /uniFB01 le has a sharper diffusion front than the H2O-limited pro /uniFB01 le, indicating that TMA is more reactive than H2O. A similar high slope was observed by Ylilammi et al .

Note that such knowledge on the reactant doses, e.g., the evolution of pressure in the reactor, can be used to analytically 14,44 estimate the penetration depths of the reactants and determine which reactant is limiting the /uniFB01 lm conformality.

FIG. Panel (a) shows thickness pro /uniFB01 les of Al2O3 deposited at different set temperatures, where the penetration depth of TMA is higher than that of H2O. For these H2O-limited pro /uniFB01 les, the temperature-dependent reactivity of H2O is re /uniFB02 ected in the increasing slope at the pro /uniFB01 le front.

These results indicate that soft-saturation during the H2O step can limit the conformality of ALD-grown Al2O3 and that a high deposition temperature or an overdose of H2O may be required to achieve optimal conformality.

This impact of soft-saturation on /uniFB01 lm conformality is typically not experimentally observed when using a structure with a relatively low AR, where the reactant dose is approximately constant throughout the feature.
